text: Hello everyone, my name is Dr. Sanket Pissatt and I am a consultant gynecological minimal access surgeon practicing in Mumbai, India. The initial part of the video is going to describe the anatomy of the female reproductive system which has the uterus in the center and a pair of tubes and ovaries on either side. In the front the uterus is related to the urinary bladder which creates the urine and behind is related to the rectum.

So there are several important vital structures which are lying in close relation to the uterus which have to be remembered during laparoscopic hysterectomy which is also called uterus removal surgery.

The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by dividing the corneal support structures at the top of the uterus using electrocautery. The same technique is applied to the corneal support structures on the opposite side. The peritoneal covering surrounding the uterus is also divided to facilitate its removal.

The surgeon divides the peritoneal covering, known as the broad ligament, on the side of the uterus. Following this, the urinary bladder is carefully separated from the anterior surface of the uterus to prevent damage during the hysterectomy procedure.

The surgeon carefully separates the urinary bladder from the anterior surface of the uterus using laparoscopic instruments. Following this, the uterine artery on the left side is divided to cut off the main blood supply to the uterus, with a similar procedure planned for the right side.
The surgeon incises the vagina, separating the cervix from the vaginal tissue, resulting in the uterus and cervix being detached with the vagina visible underneath.

The surgeon considers the removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries during the hysterectomy procedure. In younger patients, typically under fifty years of age, the ovaries are often preserved to maintain estrogen hormone secretion.

The two fallopian tubes are visibly removed from the surgical field. The surgeon then sutures the vaginal opening using an absorbable suture to reconstruct the vagina, completing the final step of the procedure.
